summary: This is an advice column ('Aunt Pythia') where Cathy O'Neil answers reader questions on academic self-doubt, dating etiquette for mathematicians, graduate school funding negotiations, and dating challenges for black gay men. The column blends humor with substantive guidance, particularly emphasizing that intelligence is cultivated through habits rather than innate gifts, and that standardized tests don't determine one's potential. O'Neil advocates for doing what you love regardless of perceived limitations, while also acknowledging systemic challenges like racial bias in dating.
key_points: Intelligence is something you do, not something you are - good habits of mind (skepticism, curiosity, humility) matter more than test scores
Students in accelerated programs often have an advantage in 'acting as if they know stuff' rather than actually knowing more
No test has the ability to determine whether someone is well-suited to a given topic - that's a personal decision
When approaching potential romantic interests in academic settings, avoid bait-and-switch tactics where math discussions become pretexts for dating
Systemic racial bias in dating is real and documented, and the practical advice is to 'play the numbers' by accessing larger, more diverse dating pools
money_quotes: You don't have to be a certified genius to be a scientist.
They are my favorite people, because they're doing what they love in spite of feeling out of place.
A couple of semesters of college is worth an entire high school career.
Nobody – and especially no test – has the ability to determine whether you are well-suited to a given topic. That's up to you to decide.
My husband thinks that intelligence is something you do, not something you are.

If you focus on good habits of mind, including being skeptical, disciplined, curious, and earnest (with a good dose of humility), then you will be far more prepared for a lifetime of science than by being anxious, competitive, or even cocky.
Being on a date is just like hanging out and talking with someone. The only real difference is, if it goes well, you can get all crushed out on them and not feel weird about it.
key_insight: Intelligence and academic success are built through deliberate habits and persistence, not predetermined by aptitude tests or natural gifts.
